pathophysiology_summary: A ureteric calculus obstructing urinary flow causes acute distension of the renal capsule and ureteric smooth muscle spasm, producing severe, colicky flank pain classically radiating along the ureteric course to the groin/testicle/labia as the stone migrates distally. Unlike peritoneal pain, ureteric colic causes the patient to writhe and constantly change position seeking relief (in contrast to the stillness of peritonitis), reflecting a visceral rather than somatic/peritoneal pain mechanism. Microscopic or macroscopic haematuria results from mucosal trauma as the stone passes. The absence of fever is reassuring against an infected, obstructed system (obstructive pyelonephritis), which would be a urological emergency requiring urgent decompression. Via telehealth, costovertebral angle tenderness and CT KUB findings cannot be obtained, but the classic historical constellation (radiating flank-to-groin pain, extreme restlessness, macroscopic haematuria, absence of fever) is sufficient to support a presumptive diagnosis of uncomplicated renal colic, while maintaining a low threshold to escalate if fever, anuria, or refractory pain develop.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden severe flank pain radiating to the groin with restlessness.

Must always be screened for in sudden severe flank/back pain, particularly in older patients, given the catastrophic consequence of missing it — though this patient's younger age and classic groin radiation with haematuria make it less likely.
Can occasionally mimic renal colic, though extreme restlessness (rather than lying still) and haematuria both argue against appendicitis.
Causes flank pain and can co-exist with an obstructing stone; must be screened for via fever, as its presence would change the urgency significantly.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The classic renal colic pattern should be leading from the opening complaint, but leaking AAA and appendicitis must still be actively screened for given the severity of the presentation, and pyelonephritis/obstruction must be screened via fever.

differential.evidence_basis: Extreme restlessness (DI-002), macroscopic haematuria (DI-003), and absence of fever (DI-004) together support an uncomplicated ureteric stone rather than a more dangerous mimic or an infected obstructed system.
Younger age, classic groin radiation, and haematuria make an AAA very unlikely, though this cannot be fully excluded without examination for a pulsatile mass.
Extreme restlessness (rather than lying still to avoid peritoneal irritation) and haematuria both argue against appendicitis.
Absence of fever or chills argues against an infected, obstructed system at this stage, though this should be explicitly safety-netted for.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage uncomplicated renal colic should be the sole leading diagnosis, and the appropriate action is urgent (same-day) in-person review for stronger analgesia and confirmation, not emergency ambulance dispatch, provided the afebrile status is confirmed and clearly safety-netted.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth context; confirmation requires non-contrast CT KUB, unavailable remotely. Urgent same-day analgesia and review is the appropriate telehealth action, with explicit safety-netting for fever/rigors.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A final plan that either dismisses the severe pain with no urgent review pathway, or over-escalates to an ambulance for an afebrile, uncomplicated-appearing presentation, both represent triage errors in opposite directions.

red_flags.description: Fever or chills (would indicate an infected, obstructed kidney) — currently absent
red_flags.clinical_significance: Absence of fever is reassuring against obstructive pyelonephritis, a urological emergency requiring urgent decompression; presence would mandate emergency escalation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If fever were present and not asked about, an infected obstructed kidney progressing to urosepsis could be missed.

red_flags.description: Costovertebral angle tenderness on percussion, absence of peritonism (clinician-only findings)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Supports the diagnosis and helps exclude a peritoneal/surgical process; requires physical examination unavailable via telehealth chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly available to the AI Doctor — reliance on the historical pattern (restlessness rather than stillness, no peritoneal symptoms volunteered) substitutes for this.

red_flags.description: Radio-opaque calculus with proximal hydroureter on CT KUB (clinician/equipment-only finding)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Confirms the diagnosis and localises the stone; requires imaging unavailable via telehealth chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly available to the AI Doctor — diagnosis remains presumptive pending in-person imaging.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-escalating to emergency ambulance for uncomplicated colic
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Given the severity of the pain, an AI Doctor might reflexively call an ambulance despite the absence of fever or other red flags — while calling 000 is not dangerous, urgent in-person review for parenteral analgesia (which cannot be given via telehealth) is the more proportionate action for a young, otherwise well patient with no signs of infection.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Under-triaging without a clear urgent-analgesia pathway
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Conversely, an AI Doctor might reassure the patient that this will pass and suggest only oral analgesia and rest at home — but oral analgesia has already failed, and the severity of ureteric colic pain typically requires parenteral analgesia unavailable outside an in-person setting, so an urgent same-day review pathway (not just reassurance) is required.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Non-contrast CT KUB for calculus and hydroureter
Vital signs (temperature, heart rate, blood pressure)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day in-person medical review (GP urgent appointment or urgent care clinic) for parenteral/stronger analgesia and to confirm the diagnosis; escalate to Emergency Department/ambulance if fever, rigors, or anuria develop
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring the patient this is 'just a stone that will pass' with oral analgesia only, when oral analgesia has already failed and parenteral analgesia is needed, leaves the patient in unnecessary severe pain and risks missing an evolving infected obstruction if fever is not actively screened for and safety-netted.
